story:
The sky hangs low and grey as weather moves across the lake toward the old cabin, a lone shape against the dark pines. Jack has been here three days, cold and hungry, savouring the silence and the way this place erases the world. There is no signal, no internet, no witnesses—only wind over water and the memory of Grandad teaching him patience and the woods’ quiet rules.

Food drives him into the trees. He moves fast before the storm turns the ground to mud, cradling the rifle he learnt to use as a boy. He waits the way Grandad taught him. A rabbit breaks cover, and the shot is clean. Back at the fire, fat spits into the pan while the rain starts, light at first, then heavy, drumming the roof like fists on a steel shell. He eats greedily, the taste sharper for the hunger, the cabin’s heat and smoke wrapping him in the old feeling of being safe while storms rage.

But safety is a story he can’t keep telling himself. He rubs at the raw blisters on his hands and thinks of why he came after twenty years away—locking his house, driving three hundred miles to vanish into the woods. It began with a letter, official and neat: divorce. Marie throwing it at his chest. Her voice rising. His refusal. The moment she said she would take their daughter, Anna, and leave. The way she added, or else, rain at the old cabin would soften the ground around Anna’s grave between the cabin and the shore. Truth, when it arrives, finds every hidden place; it stings and tears. He tells himself he was provoked, but the red haze he remembers is only a name for losing control.

The rain thickens. He steps out into it without a coat, breath steaming, and drifts to the trees between the cabin and the shore. There, a fresh mound of earth buckles the ground. Footprints circle it. The rain has started loosening the soil again. He will tidy that later, he thinks, because one mistake out here can become evidence.

Back at the car, he opens the boot and staggers at the smell. Marie is inside. The struggle was brutal, and he ended it with an axe. Anna’s death was quieter—a pillow pressed down until she stopped moving—and he had buried her on the first day, digging until his hands blistered, while Marie remained in the boot, waiting for ground he was too exhausted to break twice. He tells himself he did what he had to do because he would not be cut off from his child, but the sight of Marie’s open eyes, fixed and accusing, breaks through the story he hides behind. There is no justification here—only the weight of what he can never undo.

He slams the boot and returns to the fire. He eats the last of the rabbit, cleans the few dishes with lake water, and listens to the storm surround the cabin the way it did when he was small, when fear was only a feeling and not a fact. Tomorrow will be busy, he thinks. There are footprints to erase—the rain has softened the ground around Anna’s grave again—a body still to bury, and too many signs that he was ever here, in the old place that once meant peace. Outside, the rain hammers on. Inside, he settles into the dark, telling himself he is safe, and alone, and unseen, though the cabin already feels full of what he has done.
